sentences.text: Yes.
We'll start with my parents.
Both were born in 1901.
My sister, Lilly, who lives in Israel with her family, she was born 1923.
My brother, [?
Yaakov-- ?] Jacob-- who didn't survive, he was born 1925.
And I was the youngest.
I was born 1927.
I had a twin brother that I never knew because he died in infancy.
And--
Yes, I--

I-- when the Germans occupied us, the first thing what happened, the day they came in, the next day, they had a prepared-- already prepared list of all the Jewish leaders or important people in every Jewish community.
And they were arrested right away.
Eventually, they were let out.
When we were taken away, they came back to their families, and they went together.

Because I know one of my-- my old-- my father's oldest brother was one of those people who was arrested after they came in, but then they let him go back home, so they can go to Auschwitz together, the whole family.
And, of course, the fear was tremendous.
Everybody was afraid.
We didn't know what will happen.

In less than a month, the Germans came, like about three weeks, we were told, you can put on a wagon whatever you want from your home-- furniture, clothing, whatever you want-- and you come to the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We had a ghetto in Chust.
They-- with barbed wire, they fenced off, like, a square mile of houses, and this was the ghetto for the people from Chust and also some people from the surrounding villages.
In Chust--
In Chust, actually, there were 25,000 people, 20,000 non-Jews and 5,000 Jewish people.
And all those five-- 5,000 people had to go to the ghetto.
We really didn't know what a ghetto was till we arri-- till we-- we arrived there.
[BACKGROUND NOISE]
We were told that we can put on the wagon whatever we want to go to the ghetto.
And then we came to the ghetto.
We took the mattresses, some dishes, clothing, valuables, and all the food we had.
And everybody else did the same.
Then we came to the ghetto, and we were assigned to a certain house.
So this house happened to belong to one of our cousins.
It was like a three-bedroom home here, the size.
So when we came there, we put all the dishes and the food in the kitchen, and we were assigned to one of the bedrooms.
And when we came to the bedroom, there were already two families there.
We were 14 people in one room.
And this is what sim-- what signifies, for me, a ghetto.
So at night, we spread out the mattresses, and we slept one next to the other, the three families, and in day time, we piled them up.
And every family had a corner for their stuff.
But it was really hard to move around.
The food was delicious because the mothers alternated between themselves who will cook to show off how they can cook.
And we had enough food, and we were not hungry.
And after the dinner, we had to wash the dishes.
We were  not hungry in the ghetto because we didn't stay long enough to use up our food.
We stayed about three or three and 1/2 weeks.
So this is why our ghetto is different than the other ghettos because it went very fast.
And after two weeks, or two and 1/2 weeks, we were told, in a few days, you will leave here, and we will take you on a trip.
We will reassign you for some work.
And you can take with you not any more on wagons, just what you can carry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Before we-- yeah, and then we-- everybody started to [? solve ?] some kind of bags-- handbags, little suitcases, knapsacks, whatever and to pack.
We didn't know, from the little things we brought from home, what to take along.
But what happened during those few weeks, every day the SS used to come in and pick a group of young men for some kind of work.
And they took them behind the city and-- digging something.
I don't know exactly what work it was.
And one day, my brother was picked, too.
He was about 18 years old at that time.
And when we heard that they are taking us away the next day, and he didn't show up-- this is one of the things I will never forget.
My father was a tall man, six feet tall, very healthy, always a smile on his face, but not-- I didn't see a smile on his face since the Germans came in.
And when they advanced, and he didn't come home, he was going up and down, up and down, waiting.
He was afraid they will take us away, and he won't be with us.
Maybe it would have been for the best, but we didn't know it at that time.
Finally, it was almost dark, he came home.
And he ran to my brother, and he hugged and kissed him, and he started to cry.
And I was standing there because I never saw my father crying.
And except that he cried that he has his son back, my second thought was, would he have cried if it was me?
Because I was the youngest, and I was always kind of put down.
My sister was the oldest girl, my brother was the only boy, and this is what came to me.
So I saw my father crying.
And then, next day, we had to go to the train station.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And 5,000 people walked to the train station.
It was about two, three miles away.
You probably saw this pict-- this-- this picture in movies.
Everybody carrying whatever he could on his back and his hands, the mothers with their young children on their hands.
So the group of people were going to the train station, and not a soul was on the streets.
Nobody from the non-Jewish population came out to say a word to us, to comfort us, or to tell us, the war will end one day.
We will take care of your houses, anything.
Just like nobody existed.
But when we arrived in the-- at the station, there were a-- was a long table, and behind this table were sitting young girls taking our names and our addresses who are leaving.
And most of these girls were my former schoolmates from junior high school.
None of them, not one of them, showed a sign of recognition or sorry.
They were joking and laughing, and they couldn't care less what happens to the Jewish people.
No.
Not at all.
They were not afraid.
Afraid people don't joke and laugh like they did.
And then another thing that-- we went through a very, very personal search.
They were looking-- on our clothes, on our bodies, they were looking for valuables, jewelry, and money.
And if they found, they beat up the person except that they took away everything.
And then we were shuffled into the cattle trains.
About 80 people, give or take a few, in one car.
In my car, we had a few buckets with fresh water, and a few buckets for toilet use.
And, if we had to use it, somebody will hold up a blanket for privacy.
It was very crowded.
We sat on the floor.
There wasn't any benches or anything.
Even though we had some food with us, I always remember that my mother baked butter cookies, those big butter cookies.
And-- but nobody could eat.
And later, when I was so hungry, I always, still today-- why didn't I eat from those cookies when I had them, and I could have eaten them?
So you had butter cookies on the train?
They didn't take that away from you?
No, they didn't take away.
They were looking just for jewelry and money.
They didn't take--
Probably we had other food, but I don't-- I just remember the cookies, because they were there in a basket, and I didn't touch them all the-- all the trip.
So this bothered me later, all the time.
And today, when I go in a baker, I see the cookies, this always comes to mind.
And I never buy them.
No.
I never ate a butter cookie after the war, unless I didn't know it's a butter cookie.
I mean, those big ones.
They're called pogacsa.
In the bakers, they're called Hungarian cookies.
And then we went on the journey.
It lasted several days.
We had no idea where we are going.
And the train used to stop once a day.
It lasted about two and 1/2, three days, nights.
And when they stopped the train, they opened the doors, but before they let the boys take out-- empty out the toilet buckets and bring in fresh water, they took two hostages from every car.
And they threatened us.
If you don't give us all the money and jewelry you are hiding, we will kill those two people.
So the first time I encountered hostages.
I don't know if they killed people, because our two people always came back.
But we always heard shots ringing out, so I didn't know really what was going on somewhere else.
And then, again, the train stops, and even if it was only two and 1/2 days, it sounded like two and 1/2 years.
It felt very long, the trip.
And the train stopped, but it didn't open the doors.
And we're waiting and waiting for hours.
And the night came and the night went, and, finally, they opened the doors from the cars.
And when the opened the doors, we went to the opening.
Everybody was pushing to the opening to catch some fresh air because, by then, inside was really-- the stench, and the tiredness, and some old sick people even died in that car.
So we wanted some fresh air and to look out where we were.
I think too hot.
It was in May.
It was May-- beginning of May, 1944.
Yeah.
The Germans came in March, in April we were in the ghetto, in the beginning of May we left the ghetto, and they took us to camp.

We didn't know where we were, but when we looked out, what we saw was a lot of barbed wire, a lot of SS with their bayonets and with their machine guns, with their dogs, and also people in striped clothing.
So right away, we had a feeling, It's?
Kind of a camp.
We knew about camps from movies.
Not concentration camps, but it gave us the feeling, it's some kind of a camp, like a big ghetto, a larger ghetto than we were.
But those people in striped clothes, they all looked so small, so much smaller than the Germans.
But we still  didn't know where we were.
And then I heard my father saying to my mother-- this is the last thing I heard from him because he didn't make it.
And he said, I see people in striped clothing like in a jail.
It means they don't kill everybody.
We will obey the rules and regulations, and we will survive.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And only at that moment did I understand the fears of my father.
I was 16 and 1/2.
In some ways, I was very developed.
In some ways, I was very naive.
And I felt a kind of shame.
I don't even know.
I think it was shame.
Was I so dumb?
I didn't know what was waiting for us, and-- and maybe the first time that I was scared.
But somehow I didn't feel it because I had my strong father.
He will take care of me.
He won't let anything happen to me.
But at that moment I realized, something different is going to happen.
But I didn't have too much time to think about it because right away we were told, everybody out.
And we had to come out.
In my train, we didn't have steps, so we jumped down.
And for the older people, the pregnant women, the sick people, we helped down-- we helped down.
And when we came out of the train, we stood there.
And when I looked right and left, it was like a wall of people.
A wall of people standing there.
And this is the first picture I saw, the people standing, a wall of people, and the barbed wire.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we were told-- everybody had those bundles, little-- little, big suitcases-- put your luggage there.
And they pointed to a place, and we all went to this place to put down our bundles.
And when I came there, I saw that, like, a mountain of luggages, all shapes, sizes, and colors.
And when I put down mine, I said, oh, how will I recognize it?
I didn't put my name on it?
Of course, I never had this problem, to recognize it.
So there we are standing, just the clothes on our body, and we went back at the previous position to stand next to the train.
And then came the first real order from the Germans and the first separation of families.
Men here and women here!
Schnell, schnell, they shouted.
And from that moment, until the liberation, every order was given in German, and every time they added a million times the schnell, fast.
They always pushed us, fast, fast, so we wouldn't have time to think or to do something else.
But, in spite of everything, eventually we learned to do other things than they told us in order to save our lives.
So, suddenly, we find ourselves separated from the families.
We still could see each other, and everybody's trying to talk.
We didn't say anything to each other.
We didn't know we would be separated.
And here we are standing, and we started to shout at each other.
But, if you can imagine, 5,000 people, everybody was shouting, everybody's talking.
Nobody could hear or understand one word.
So we never said anything to each other, that the war will end, where we would meet, nothing.
And this was the last time that I saw my father and my brother.
And then we were a huge group of women with all the small children, and we have to go, just told to go.
And we still didn't know where we were.
And we started to walk.
While we were walking to any-- to some place that we later arrived, two things happened.
One thing was that those people-- those people in striped clothing, I called them the old timers.
They-- their job was, in this camp, to tell the trainload of people arrived-- they called it 'a new transport arrived.' So this was their job, to tell us to put the luggage, to go here, to go there.
Give-- give the young children, give the babies, to the older people, to the grandmothers, to the aunts.
So the mothers ask why.
They said, you will go to work.
They will take care of the children.
So some mothers accepted it and gave up the children.
Most of them didn't.
And later we-- we found out what-- I will come back to this because I want to go on what happened to us.
The other announcement was made, in German, that all the twins step out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And not only I was from twins, but my mother was from twins, too.
But she also lost her twin brother in babyhood, so we just know that generation and generation we had twins.
We had no idea why they are saying, step out.
But we looked at each other, and it's a-- it's a miracle for me till today.
We looked at each other, the three of us, we didn't say one word to each other, and we didn't step out.
And this was really the first link of-- of-- of the chain of our survival.
We did not step out as twins.
And then we went on, and then, suddenly, I saw a big sign-- Auschwitz.
I had no idea what it meant.
I didn't know, at that moment, that this will be a name that I will remember forever.
And under this name was a whole sentence, like an arch, that said, 'Arbeit Macht Frei.' Work makes you free.
So, of course, eventually, we found out that we are in Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we progressed in our walk, and then I saw, in front me, a group of German officers, and soldiers, and men and women in striped clothing.
And this offer, one of the officers, stood out especially.
The first thing what came to my mind when I saw him, I said, oh, my God, is he handsome.
I never saw a more handsome man than he is.
I was 16 and 1/2, and that's what I saw when I saw him first.
And he was standing there in his uniform.
He had on white gloves, and he had a little stick, and he was pointing.
When we came close to him, pointing right and left, life and death.
He decided who will live and who will die.
And this was the famous Dr.
Mengele.
This was the first time I saw him, but not the last time.
And then later, we found out that he made experiments on human beings and on twins, and we were very lucky we didn't step out.
Another thing that I remember from this situation when we were walking-- one of my aunts with her teenage daughters was in front of us.
And she was-- I always knew she's 10 years older than my mother.
So if my mother was 43, she had to be in her early 50s.
And somehow, when she-- Mengele didn't talk too often, but once in a while, he talked, and this was one of those rare occasions.
And, I always say, he even touched me once.
Later when we're at other selections, once he touched me.
So he looked at my aunt, and he said, how old are you?
And she looked back at him, and she snapped back, 35.
And he let her go with her daughters.
And one of her daughters just visited me a few weeks ago, and I told her.
She didn't-- doesn't remember it, and I know why.
I remember because she said 35, and I knew she was older.
So this is why I remember this incident.
But what I'm trying to say here-- her mind worked so fast.
She was a very bright woman.
She survived.
She died already in New York.
That she knew that 35, for whatever reason, sounds so much better than to say 50.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
And another aunt who was with her five younger kids from eight-- she didn't give up her kids to the grandmother, and she was sent to the left.
And all those children, all those people who-- yeah, I forgot to say that Mengele was pointing right and left.
Right were a certain group of people-- some 12 or 13-year-old girls who looked older, some 50-year-old women who looked younger-- went to the living group.
And all the other people-- old people, pregnant women who showed, all the mothers with their young children-- were to the left.
And they were all gassed and cremated the same day.
So when those people, those old-timers, when they told, give the young children to the older people, they knew what will happen to the mothers with the young children.
What they tried do, they tried to save some of those young mothers who were in their 20 or 30s, because this was the only way to do it.
And then we heard that previous times, Mengele wa-- picked the young mothers to the living, then they had to grab the children, and it started a commotion.
They screamed, and they shouted, and they-- they cried.
They wouldn't let the children go.
So he decided, I don't want a commotion.
Let the mother go with the children.
So this is what really happened, what the old timers told us.
Then we came to the entlousing building, and there everything went very fast.
The first-- To which building?
Entlousing.
Delousing.
I mix up.
Delousing building, that's right.
Not really.
Not-- yeah, I will tell you about it.
So I don't know what-- what they were searching, but obviously to make us clean, for whatever reason.
First, we came in one room, and we were told, undress completely.
Keep only your shoes.
And suddenly, we're undressed and we're standing, hundreds of women, naked.
And it was very, very embarrassing, but that wasn't all.
The old timer men and the SS men were walking around with naked women very naturally.
Of course, later we found out that it was an everyday scene for them, but it wasn't for us.
And we were so embarrassed.
We tried to hide between each other, but it was nothing we could do.
Then we went to the next room, and there was a group of girls-- old timers-- and they were sitting on chairs.
No, they were standing there with chairs, and they shaved our hair from our head, and other parts of the body, too.
So here we were, naked, without hair, only with our shoes.
Then we went to the third part, and we were told, put your shoes at the walls and go in the middle.
And we went in the middle, and we were the lucky ones.
We received some lukewarm water.
Because later, we found out that the other people who went to left, they went through the same procedure, but their doors were hermetically closed, and they got the Zyklon gas, not the water.
After this we went to the next, went outside, we put on our shoes, on our bare feet.
We received one set of underwear, a group of girls, [INAUDIBLE]
No.
No.
Before we received our clothing, there were a group of girls with-- disinfecting us with those little machines under our arms and other places, so we will be real clean.
Then, the next place, a group of girls, it was their job.
We received one set of underwear and one dress, a regular summer dress.
They didn't have any more striped clothing, so we didn't have striped clothing.
And then we looked at each other, and we had to hold on because we were hundreds-- thousands-- of people with strange clothes, without hair, and it was very easy to get lost.
And then to find someone in the crowds was impossible.
So the three of us are holding onto each other-- mother, Lilly, and I.
And so did other sisters and the few mothers who were-- because not too many people had the mothers with them.
We were just the lucky ones, our age level, that we were together.
And then we were taken, given over to a block alteste, and taken to Camp C.
I remember, at Camp C, they had 30 barracks, a thousand-- about 1,000 people in one barracks.
Our barracks was number 20C-- 26.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The block alteste was named Itel.
She was one of the cruel, mean peo-- persons I met during my stay in concentration camp.
She was very jealous, and she told us-- she gave us a speech when we arrived-- she said, you were still at home with your parents.
You still had enough food.
You were warm, and I was already here.
You have a [NON-ENGLISH], you have a-- a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] leisure place, according what I had when I came to Auschwitz.
And then she  told us, in the gate you came in-- and she pointed to the chimney-- the chimneys you will go out.
And when we turned to the chimneys, it was the first time I realized that those are crematoriums.
And even though I come from a small town in Czechoslovakia, we had a crematorium in our city.
In certain-- in our town.
We had a gymnasium, we had a crematorium.
So I knew what it is.
Non-Jewish people used to be cremated, even before World War II.
So when she mentioned the crematoriums, I knew exactly what she meant.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it really scared us.
No.
She was a-- from Slovensko, a  Jew.
And she was there already for three-- from the beginning, when they started to build Auschwitz.
Itel.
That's all that I know.
Itel.
Similar to my name, [?
Ethel. ?] Maybe in her place they called her Itel.
Yeah, Camp C.
Camp C, barrack 26, and her name was Itel.
And we suffered so much that, later, when we found other people-- relatives, friends-- outside, they said, why do you stay there?
Because we didn't have any number, so we couldn't move around.
So then, eventually, we dared to move to another barracks so we will go away from her.
Because once, what she did-- once in a while they used to change our clothes, once in a few months.
So, again, when she changed the clothes, my mother received a very small dress.
And we told her to stay in line again, and she will receive another.
And she saw my mother, and she came up, and she smacked her in her face.
And her lips were-- she had sunburn on her lips, and she started to bleed.
And the blood was just gushing from her mouth.
And we started to scream, Lilly and I.
What did you do to my mother?
Why did you do it?
And she had-- one of her-- her sister came with our transport, and she was with her.
And her sister ran to her little-- she had a little room, separately-- and brought a dish with water and a weiss-- piece of white cloth, and she helped wash my mother's blood.
So those were two sisters in a different approach to us.
[INAUDIBLE] sister.
What-- what do you-- what do you think brought Itel to this kind of behavior?
It brought her to the behavior because we were taught to stay in line and to get a dress.
We were not supposed to go again.
And we told her, but you gave mother such as small dress.
She didn't care.
She acted-- at that point, she acted like the Germans, and she didn't have to.
But at that time that she came, it was terrible, so the jealousy.
And she let out her jealousy to us.
She didn't have anybody else to let it out.
So she let it out.
I think people react on bad experiences in life in two ways.
Or they are kinder to other people afterwards, because they-- what they went through, or they want to pay back to somebody else.
And I think she belonged to those who wanted to pay back.
This was my worst experience.
I didn't have too-- too many wrong because I usually was very careful to-- with me-- my behavior.
And I was very lucky.
I was really, except one time, never singled out on my own to-- to have punishment.
But once, when I volunteered to go to-- to-- for some kind of work, I was taken to-- this was already in the second camp-- I was taken to Leipzig to pick up something on trucks.
And I succeeded to grab some potatoes that I put in-- under my dress.
And when we came back to camp, I ran to my barracks to hide it.
And when I came back, the SS woman saw me.
She said, where did you go?
And she slapped me on my face real hard because I was not supposed to leave the pla-- the group.
She was responsible for a certain amount of people.
And I was crying so bitterly.
Not that it hurt me so much, but I felt I didn't deserve to be slapped.
But this was really the only time that I was singled out.
And-- and I really was very careful always to-- to stay in a group.
It was-- was more safe to be in a group than separately.
Probably two, three months.
I don't remember.
Yeah.
For instance, when we were taken up to-- for [INAUDIBLE], for being counted, we were waken up in the middle of the night.
I will never know if it
00 or 3:00 or 4:00 at night.
So she said, [INAUDIBLE], raus.
Out, out, everybody.
And then she will stay in the opened door-- it was big doors-- with a stick.
And when the people pushed out, she was standing there and hitting, hitting everybody.
So everybody was afraid, so everybody tried to be in the middle.
So the pushing and the shouting was so great, that it was-- it was impossible.
